binaries.title: ci.dll (Windows Code Integrity) - SI policy platform-secured trust-check tightened; one policy-ID Windows-signing mandate relaxed

changed_functions.name: SIPolicyIsPolicyPlatformSecured (sub_18006FC88)
key_changes: REMOVED early-return on the arg2==0 path when (result & 4) that returned 1 (platform secured) immediately
Patched version on the arg2==0 path now requires (result & 1) set AND a hardcoded 16-byte policy-identity at +0x6CC/+0x6D4 to match before returning 1
The arg2!=0 && (result & 0x18) early return is preserved in both versions; flag offset +0x710 is unchanged
changed_functions.security_notes: SIPolicyIsPolicyPlatformSecured determines whether a loaded System Integrity policy is 'platform secured' (return 1). It reads a policy-options flag dword at *(arg1+0x710). In the unpatched version, when called with arg2=0 (non-strict path), the function immediately returns 1 if bit 2 (value & 4) is set, skipping the stricter identity check. This is a trust-check bypass: a policy is treated as platform-secured on the strength of a single flag bit. The patched version removes this shortcut, so on the arg2=0 path it requires bit 0 set AND the fixed policy-identity value 0x4C0644C9A244370E / 0x7630566E01F651B5 at offset +0x6CC/+0x6D4 to match. There is no page-hash table or binary search in this function.

changed_functions.name: SIPolicyIsPolicyMustBeWindowsSigned (sub_18006FB74)
key_changes: REMOVED one entry (0x4C3279F4784C4414 a1[0] / 0xD1DA542FBF0A5A6 a1[1], offset +0x8) from the hardcoded list of policy IDs that must be Windows-signed. Unpatched has 9 entries, patched has 8; all other entries are unchanged

Direction is a RELAXATION: in both builds the caller reaches the customized-signed acceptance path only when the policy is not Windows-signed AND this function returns 0, so a listed identity is barred from customized signing and must be Windows-signed. Removing the entry lets that identity be accepted via customized signing in the patched build (patched is less strict)
changed_functions.security_notes: SIPolicyIsPolicyMustBeWindowsSigned compares a 16-byte policy identity against a built-in list of hardcoded policy-identity constants and returns 1 on a match. Verified in the decompilation: unpatched lists 9 identity pairs, patched lists 8, and the removed pair is 0x4C3279F4784C4414 / 0xD1DA542FBF0A5A6. The sole caller uses a match to BLOCK the customized-signed (non-Windows) acceptance path, so removal relaxes the Windows-signing mandate for that identity rather than revoking trust. This is a policy-ID list adjustment, not an unpatched-vulnerable/patched-fixed security change; the unpatched build is the stricter one here.

changed_functions.name: CiHvciIsVbsPolicyFailure (sub_18006C428)
key_changes: Patched adds a feature-gated pre-check via Feature_CodeIntegrity_TrustedLaunchPolicy__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsageNoInline (sub_180018DF8)
When that feature is enabled, returns 0 (no VBS policy failure) unless bit 0x100000 is set and bit 0x200000 is clear in *(arg1+0x938); offset +0x938 is unchanged between builds
changed_functions.security_notes: CiHvciIsVbsPolicyFailure is a standalone HVCI/VBS policy-failure detector that walks loaded policies looking for a specific hardcoded policy identity (0x4B5E588BA072029F / 0x87F67DD6AA05F9B7). It does NOT call SIPolicyIsPolicyMustBeWindowsSigned; it is not the caller of the hardcoded-identity allowlist. The patch adds a feature-gated flag check on the flags at +0x938 that further constrains when a VBS policy failure is reported.

changed_functions.name: I_FindFileOrHeaderHashInLoadedCatalogs (sub_1800D6FEC)
key_changes: SHA-256 branch (a3==32780) changed bsearch to bsearch_s (secure variant requiring context parameter), using CipFileHashSearchCompareRoutineSHA256
SHA-1 branch (a3==32772) continues to use bsearch in both builds
changed_functions.security_notes: I_FindFileOrHeaderHashInLoadedCatalogs looks up a file/header hash in the sorted hash tables of loaded catalogs. The unpatched SHA-256 lookup uses bsearch; the patched version switches that call to bsearch_s, which adds parameter validation. This is a hardening fix for potential out-of-bounds access during binary search of catalog hash table data.

changed_functions.name: SbpParseSignatureDatabase (unpatched 0x18000BDDC) / SbpParseSignatureDatabaseFromBuffer (patched-only 0x18001E1DC)
key_changes: The EFI Secure Boot signature-database parser was refactored: unpatched SbpParseSignatureDatabase @0x18000BDDC did the merged-list allocation inline; the patch splits the parsing/allocation logic into a newly added helper SbpParseSignatureDatabaseFromBuffer @0x18001E1DC, called from the patched SbpParseSignatureDatabase @0x18000E0FC

Unpatched computes the allocation size with raw shift/add and no overflow check: lea rcx,[r14+rdi]; shl rcx,4; add rcx,r15; call SbeAlloc (@0x18000BE49-0x18000BE54), then copies attacker EFI-variable data with memmove (@0x18000BF62). RtlULongLongMult does not appear anywhere in the unpatched binary

Patched replaces each *16 with checked RtlULongLongMult (multiplier 0x10) tested via test eax,eax; js loc_18001E5BC, and each region addition with an explicit carry check (cmp r10,[rbp+pullResult]; jb loc_18001E574 -> STATUS_INTEGER_OVERFLOW 0xC0000095) before SbeAlloc (@0x18001E2EC) and memmove (@0x18001E324)
changed_functions.security_notes: SbpParseSignatureDatabase retrieves an EFI (UEFI) Secure Boot signature-database variable via SbEFIGetVariable (@0x18000BE21 in the unpatched build) and parses its EFI_SIGNATURE_LIST records to build a merged descriptor array. It is reached from SbParseKEK @0x18001E16C, which parses Secure Boot KEK/db variable data - untrusted firmware/Secure-Boot input. The unpatched build computes the merged-list allocation size with raw shl/add and no overflow validation, then memmoves attacker-controlled variable data into the result. The patch validates every multiplication with RtlULongLongMult and every addition with a carry check before allocating, returning STATUS_INTEGER_OVERFLOW on failure. Direction is correct (patched stricter). Rated Low: the guarded operands are 32-bit entry counts times 16 added to a 32-bit variable size, computed in 64-bit registers and bounded by the parsed variable size, so a full 64-bit overflow of the allocation size (and the undersized-alloc-plus-memmove heap overflow it would cause) is not demonstrable from these binaries alone. Best characterized as CWE-190 integer-overflow hardening (defense-in-depth) guarding a CWE-787/CWE-122 heap buffer overflow on an untrusted UEFI-variable parsing path.
changed_functions.assembly_diff:
// UNPATCHED ci!SbpParseSignatureDatabase @ 0x18000BDDC (no overflow check):
000000018000BE21  call    SbEFIGetVariable
// ... counting pass yields entry counts in r14 / rdi (r15d) ...
000000018000BE49  lea     rcx, [r14+rdi]   // count_a + count_b
000000018000BE4D  shl     rcx, 4           // * 16, raw, unchecked
000000018000BE51  add     rcx, r15         // + Size, raw, unchecked
000000018000BE54  call    SbeAlloc
000000018000BF62  call    memmove          // copies attacker EFI-variable data

// PATCHED ci!SbpParseSignatureDatabaseFromBuffer @ 0x18001E1DC (checked):
000000018001E261  mov     edx, 10h         // ullMultiplier = 16
000000018001E269  call    RtlULongLongMult
000000018001E270  js      loc_18001E5BC    // overflow -> free + return
000000018001E287  call    RtlULongLongMult
000000018001E290  js      loc_18001E5BC
000000018001E29A  add     r10, [rbp+pullResult]
000000018001E29E  cmp     r10, [rbp+pullResult]
000000018001E2A2  jb      loc_18001E574    // carry -> STATUS_INTEGER_OVERFLOW 0xC0000095
000000018001E2B7  call    RtlULongLongMult
000000018001E2C0  js      loc_18001E5BC
000000018001E2CA  add     rdx, r10
000000018001E2CD  cmp     rdx, r10
000000018001E2D0  jb      loc_18001E574
000000018001E2D9  lea     rcx, [rdx+r14]   // + Size
000000018001E2DD  cmp     rcx, rdx
000000018001E2E0  jb      loc_18001E574
000000018001E2EC  call    SbeAlloc         // only after all checks pass
000000018001E324  call    memmove

changed_functions.name: wil_details_FeatureStateCache_ReevaluateCachedFeatureEnabledState (sub_180018FC8)
key_changes: Patched adds | 0x40000 when computing the cached feature-state value (bit 18 of the WIL feature-state cache word)
This reflects a feature-flag rollout/encoding change in the WIL feature-state cache, not a code-integrity verification flag
changed_functions.security_notes: This is the WIL (Windows Implementation Library) feature-state cache reevaluation helper; the 0x40000 bit is part of the cached feature-enabled-state encoding. It is a feature-flag rollout change rather than a code-integrity security check.

changed_functions.name: SymCryptFdefDecideModulusType (sub_18000E878)
key_changes: Patched adds a (v & 0xC000) == 0 guard combined with a CPU-feature check before selecting the modulus type
Statically-linked SymCrypt library math routine, unrelated to Code Integrity trust logic
changed_functions.security_notes: SymCryptFdefDecideModulusType is a statically-linked SymCrypt library routine that chooses an internal modulus representation based on CPU features. The change is a CPU-feature-gating tweak in that library, not a Code Integrity security check.

changed_functions.name: I_ParseCatalogAndMapHashes (sub_1800882F4)
key_changes: Inlined I_AllocateCatalogParseBuffers (sub_1800884A0), a pool allocator for a 0xed0-byte structure, directly into the function body
I_AllocateCatalogParseBuffers existed in the unpatched version with the same 0xed0 allocation size
Not a security fix - the allocation size is identical (0xed0 bytes) in both versions
changed_functions.security_notes: Initially appeared to be a stack-to-pool overflow fix, but I_AllocateCatalogParseBuffers in the unpatched binary already allocates the same 0xed0-byte pool. The change is purely structural refactoring (inlining).

changed_functions.name: CiQueryInformation (sub_1800D50A0)
key_changes: Patched adds condition checking (data_180045e70 & 0x400000) before setting a verification flag
Gates the flag on an additional global configuration bit

changed_functions.name: SIPolicyParsePolicyData (sub_18006E09C)
key_changes: Struct offset shift: +0xa80 -> +0xaa0 reflecting expanded context structure layout
Added VBS capability check via sub_180018df8() before certain operations

changed_functions.name: CipValidateFileHash (sub_1800DB310)
key_changes: Struct offset shifts across multiple field accesses
Added VBS gating and additional flag checks

changed_functions.name: CiUnpackPolicy (sub_1800AD8EC)
key_changes: Both builds call memmove (sub_180029280 in unpatched, sub_180029880 in patched are both memmove); the symbol difference is only an address shift from the overall binary layout change, not a memcpy-to-memmove switch
changed_functions.security_notes: The referenced copy-routine symbol resolves to memmove in both builds; the address changed only because the binary shifted. No memcpy-to-memmove substitution occurred. Behavioral only.

changed_functions.name: CipMinCryptToAuthRoot2 (sub_180054BC4)
key_changes: Extensive refactoring with VBS checks added throughout
Policy verification logic changes for HVCI compatibility

added: SbpParseSignatureDatabaseFromBuffer (0x18001E1DC) - patched-only helper extracted from SbpParseSignatureDatabase; adds RtlULongLongMult/carry-check integer-overflow validation to the EFI Secure Boot signature-database allocation-size computation (see Finding 4)

security_findings.entry_point: System Integrity policy evaluation (SIPolicy platform-secured determination)
security_findings.vulnerability_class: trust bypass / missing validation (CWE-358: Improperly Implemented Security Check)
security_findings.description: The unpatched SIPolicyIsPolicyPlatformSecured function decides whether a loaded System Integrity policy is 'platform secured' (return 1). When called with arg2=0 (the non-strict path), it reads a policy-options flag dword from *(arg1 + 0x710) and if bit 2 (value & 4) is set, it immediately returns 1 (platform secured) without performing the stricter identity check. This means a policy is treated as platform-secured on the strength of one flag bit. The patched version removes this early-return shortcut so that on the arg2=0 path the function requires bit 0 (value & 1) to be set AND a hardcoded 16-byte policy identity (0x4C0644C9A244370E / 0x7630566E01F651B5) at offset +0x6CC/+0x6D4 to match before returning 1. This function contains no page-hash table and no binary search. Verified against both binaries (decompilation and assembly). Rated Medium rather than High: the 'platform secured' result is a real trust gate (one caller honors a policy's PlatformSecureSetting query only for platform-secured policies; another proceeds with image validation once a loaded policy is platform-secured), but end-to-end exploitability requires an attacker-loadable policy carrying +0x710 bit 2 without the fixed identity, which is not demonstrable from these binaries alone.
security_findings.trigger_conditions: The weakness triggers when: (1) an SI policy is loaded/evaluated whose flag dword at +0x710 has bit 2 set but which lacks the hardcoded identity at +0x6CC/+0x6D4, and (2) a caller invokes SIPolicyIsPolicyPlatformSecured with arg2=0. Under these conditions the unpatched build reports the policy as platform-secured without the identity check.
security_findings.primitive: SI-policy trust-state bypass - a policy is classified as 'platform secured' (a trusted state downstream code relies on) using only flag bit 2, without the fixed policy-identity match. This is a logic/trust weakness, not a direct code-execution primitive.

breakpoints.why: Break at function entry to observe the SI policy pointer (arg1/rcx) and the arg2 flag (dl). When dl==0 and [rcx+0x710] bit 2 is set, the unpatched function returns 1 without the identity check.
loc_18006FCDD: the 'test al, 4' shortcut. If execution sets al=1 here and returns without reaching the identity compare at +0x6CC/+0x6D4, the shortcut is active.

watch: rcx = pointer to SI policy structure
[rcx+0x710] = policy-options flags; bit 2 (value & 4) triggers the shortcut when dl==0
dl (arg2) = strict flag; shortcut only occurs when dl==0
[rcx+0x28] = count field feeding the +0x6CC/+0x6D4 offset selection
key_offsets: [rcx+0x710]: policy-options flags containing the shortcut bit
The 'test al, 4; jz; mov al,1; retn' sequence at loc_18006FCDD is the removed shortcut
[rcx+0x6CC]/[rcx+0x6D4]: hardcoded policy-identity compared on the full path
debugging_aids.trigger_setup: Arrange for an SI policy to be loaded/evaluated so that SIPolicyIsPolicyPlatformSecured is invoked with arg2=0. Trace into the function and inspect the flag dword at +0x710.
debugging_aids.expected_observation: In the unpatched binary: dl==0 (arg2), [rcx+0x710] has bit 2 set, and the function returns al=1 without reaching the identity compare. In the patched binary the same input falls through to require bit 0 and the fixed identity at +0x6CC/+0x6D4, returning 0 when those do not hold.
debugging_aids.notes: The flag offset is +0x710 in BOTH builds (there is no shift to +0x720). This function is part of the SI policy subsystem in ci.dll and decides the 'platform secured' trust state of a policy; it does not validate page hashes.

security_findings.entry_point: System Integrity policy evaluation (hardcoded Windows-signing mandate list)
security_findings.vulnerability_class: signing-requirement relaxation of a hardcoded policy-ID list (no unpatched-vulnerable / patched-fixed direction)
security_findings.description: SIPolicyIsPolicyMustBeWindowsSigned compares a 16-byte policy identity against a built-in list of hardcoded policy-identity constants and returns 1 on a match. Verified in decompilation: unpatched lists 9 identity pairs, patched lists 8, and the removed pair is 0x4C3279F4784C4414 (a1[0]) / 0xD1DA542FBF0A5A6 (a1[1], offset +0x8). The DIRECTION is a relaxation, not a revocation: in both builds the sole caller reaches the customized-signed acceptance path (SIPolicyCheckPolicyCustomizedSigned, allowing a non-Windows signer) only when the policy is not Windows-signed (status 0xC0430005) AND this function returns 0. A listed identity is therefore barred from customized signing and must be Windows-signed; removing the entry lets that identity be accepted via customized signing in the patched build, so the patched build is the less strict one. This is a policy-ID list adjustment, not an unpatched-vulnerable/patched-fixed security change. The separately-changed CiHvciIsVbsPolicyFailure (sub_18006C428) is an unrelated HVCI/VBS policy-failure detector and does not call this function.
security_findings.trigger_conditions: A policy whose 16-byte identity equals 0x4C3279F4784C4414 (a1[0]) / 0xD1DA542FBF0A5A6 (a1[1], offset +0x8) is forced by the unpatched ci.dll to be Windows-signed; the patched build no longer forces this and permits that identity through the customized-signed acceptance path.
security_findings.primitive: None (relaxation). Removing the entry lifts a Windows-signing requirement for one policy identity; it does not grant an attacker a new primitive in the patched build.

breakpoints.why: Break at the policy-identity allowlist compare. Observe arg1/rcx pointing to the 16-byte identity. Watch for the comparison against constant 0x4C3279F4784C4414.

Unrelated HVCI/VBS policy-failure detector. In the patched build, observe the feature-gated flag check (Feature_CodeIntegrity_TrustedLaunchPolicy..., sub_180018DF8) on flags at +0x938 that can force a no-failure return.

watch: rcx = pointer to the 16-byte policy identity being compared
al return value: 1=identity is on the must-be-Windows-signed list (forced Windows-signing), 0=not on the list
key_offsets: The removed allowlist entry compares a1[0] (offset +0x0) and a1[1] (offset +0x8)
debugging_aids.trigger_setup: Evaluate a policy whose identity matches the removed constant and observe whether the function returns 1.
debugging_aids.expected_observation: In unpatched ci.dll the identity matches 0x4C3279F4784C4414 / 0xD1DA542FBF0A5A6 and returns 1 (forcing Windows-signing). In patched ci.dll that entry is absent, the function returns 0 for that identity, and the caller may accept it via the customized-signed path.
debugging_aids.notes: This removes a Windows-signing mandate for one policy identity (a relaxation); the unpatched build is stricter. The CiHvciIsVbsPolicyFailure change is separate and constrains when a VBS policy failure is reported under HVCI (feature-gated flag check at +0x938, offset unchanged).

security_findings.entry_point: Catalog hash table binary search during file/header hash lookup
security_findings.vulnerability_class: potential out-of-bounds read / improper bounds handling (CWE-125), addressed as defensive hardening
security_findings.description: I_FindFileOrHeaderHashInLoadedCatalogs looks up a file/header hash in the sorted hash tables of loaded catalogs. Verified in decompilation: the unpatched SHA-256 branch (a3==32780) uses bsearch() with element size 0x20 and comparator I_HashSearchCompareRoutineSHA256; the patched version switches that call to bsearch_s() with CipFileHashSearchCompareRoutineSHA256, element size 0x20, and a null context argument, adding runtime parameter validation. The SHA-1 branch (a3==32772, element size 0x14) continues to use bsearch in both builds. This is a defensive hardening change; no out-of-bounds condition is demonstrated in either build.
security_findings.trigger_conditions: Requires a malformed catalog hash table entry or an inconsistent count/size that causes bsearch to traverse beyond allocated bounds during the binary search.
security_findings.primitive: Potential out-of-bounds read of kernel pool memory during catalog hash table lookup

breakpoints.why: Catalog hash lookup function. Watch the SHA-256 branch call to bsearch (unpatched) vs bsearch_s (patched) and the table pointer/count parameters.

watch: element size (0x20 for SHA-256, 0x14 for SHA-1)
key_offsets: SHA-256 bsearch->bsearch_s call site within I_FindFileOrHeaderHashInLoadedCatalogs
debugging_aids.trigger_setup: Validate a file whose hash is looked up in loaded catalogs to trigger the SHA-256 catalog hash table search.
debugging_aids.expected_observation: Unpatched calls bsearch on the SHA-256 branch; patched calls bsearch_s with context. No crash expected under normal conditions.
debugging_aids.notes: Hardening fix. Low exploitability but worth noting as defense-in-depth.

security_findings.entry_point: EFI Secure Boot signature-database variable parsing (SbParseKEK -> SbpParseSignatureDatabase -> SbEFIGetVariable)
call_chain: SbParseKEK (0x18001E16C) (parses the KEK/db Secure Boot variable)
SbpParseSignatureDatabaseFromBuffer (0x18001E1DC) (patched-only; overflow-checked allocation-size computation before SbeAlloc/memmove)
security_findings.vulnerability_class: integer overflow in size computation (CWE-190) guarding an undersized-allocation heap buffer overflow (CWE-787 / CWE-122), addressed as defensive hardening
security_findings.description: SbpParseSignatureDatabase retrieves an EFI (UEFI) Secure Boot signature-database variable via SbEFIGetVariable (@0x18000BE21 in the unpatched build) and parses its EFI_SIGNATURE_LIST records to build a merged descriptor array. It is reached from SbParseKEK (@0x18001E16C), which parses the Secure Boot KEK/db variable contents - firmware/Secure-Boot configuration data that is exactly the untrusted input this parser validates. In the unpatched build the merged-list allocation size is computed with raw shift/add and no overflow check: after the counting pass the entry counts (r14, rdi) and the raw variable size (r15) are combined as lea rcx,[r14+rdi]; shl rcx,4; add rcx,r15 and passed straight to SbeAlloc (@0x18000BE54), after which the attacker-controlled variable data is copied in with memmove (@0x18000BF62). RtlULongLongMult does not appear anywhere in the unpatched binary. The patch extracts this logic into the new helper SbpParseSignatureDatabaseFromBuffer and replaces every *16 with a checked RtlULongLongMult (multiplier 0x10) tested via test eax,eax; js loc_18001E5BC, and follows every region addition with an explicit carry check (cmp r10,[rbp+pullResult]; jb loc_18001E574, where loc_18001E574 sets STATUS_INTEGER_OVERFLOW 0xC0000095) before SbeAlloc (@0x18001E2EC) and memmove (@0x18001E324). Direction is correct (patched stricter). Verified against both binaries. Rated Low: the guarded operands are 32-bit entry counts multiplied by 16 and added to a 32-bit variable size, computed in 64-bit registers and bounded by the parsed variable size, so a full 64-bit overflow of the allocation size - and the undersized-allocation-plus-memmove heap overflow it would cause - is not demonstrable from these two binaries alone. Best characterized as CWE-190 integer-overflow hardening (defense-in-depth) that forecloses an undersized SbeAlloc followed by an oversized memmove of attacker-controlled EFI-variable data.
security_findings.trigger_conditions: An attacker who can influence the size/entry-count fields of a Secure Boot signature-database (KEK/db/dbx) UEFI variable that SbpParseSignatureDatabase parses. In the unpatched build the allocation size is computed without any RtlULongLongMult/carry check, so an oversized combination of counts and size would flow directly into SbeAlloc and a subsequent memmove of attacker data.
security_findings.primitive: Potential integer-overflow -> undersized heap allocation -> heap buffer overflow via memmove of attacker-controlled EFI Secure Boot variable data. Not demonstrable as a reachable 64-bit overflow from these binaries because the multiplicands are 32-bit counts times 16 added to a 32-bit variable size, so the change is best treated as defense-in-depth integer-overflow hardening.
security_findings.evidence:
// UNPATCHED ci!SbpParseSignatureDatabase @ 0x18000BDDC (no overflow check):
000000018000BE21  call    SbEFIGetVariable
000000018000BE49  lea     rcx, [r14+rdi]   // count_a + count_b
000000018000BE4D  shl     rcx, 4           // * 16, raw, unchecked
000000018000BE51  add     rcx, r15         // + Size, raw, unchecked
000000018000BE54  call    SbeAlloc
000000018000BF62  call    memmove

// PATCHED ci!SbpParseSignatureDatabaseFromBuffer @ 0x18001E1DC (checked):
000000018001E269  call    RtlULongLongMult
000000018001E270  js      loc_18001E5BC
000000018001E29A  add     r10, [rbp+pullResult]
000000018001E29E  cmp     r10, [rbp+pullResult]
000000018001E2A2  jb      loc_18001E574    // STATUS_INTEGER_OVERFLOW 0xC0000095
000000018001E2EC  call    SbeAlloc
000000018001E324  call    memmove
security_findings.assembly:
// UNPATCHED @0x18000BDDC: lea rcx,[r14+rdi]; shl rcx,4; add rcx,r15; call SbeAlloc; ... call memmove -- no overflow validation.
// PATCHED @0x18001E1DC: RtlULongLongMult (multiplier 0x10) with 'test eax,eax; js loc_18001E5BC' after each multiply, and 'cmp <sum>,<prev>; jb loc_18001E574' carry check after each add, before SbeAlloc/memmove.

breakpoints.why: Unpatched entry. After SbEFIGetVariable (0x18000BE21) fills [rbp+Src]/[rbp+Size], break at 0x18000BE49 to observe the raw lea/shl/add allocation-size computation fed into SbeAlloc at 0x18000BE54 with no overflow check.

Patched helper. Break at the RtlULongLongMult calls (0x18001E269, 0x18001E287, 0x18001E2B7) and the carry checks (0x18001E29E, 0x18001E2CD, 0x18001E2DD) to observe the overflow validation before SbeAlloc (0x18001E2EC) and memmove (0x18001E324).

watch: [rbp+Src] / [rbp+Size] = attacker-controlled EFI Secure Boot variable buffer and size (from SbEFIGetVariable)
r14 / rdi = accumulated EFI_SIGNATURE_LIST entry counts (unpatched)
rcx at 0x18000BE51 = computed allocation size (count_sum*16 + Size), unchecked in unpatched
eax after each RtlULongLongMult (patched) = NTSTATUS; sign bit set (js) means overflow/error
[rbp+pullResult] (patched) = running size accumulator checked for carry before SbeAlloc
key_offsets: 0x18000BE49-0x18000BE54: unpatched raw lea/shl/add + SbeAlloc (no overflow check)
debugging_aids.trigger_setup: Arrange for a Secure Boot signature-database (KEK/db/dbx) UEFI variable whose EFI_SIGNATURE_LIST entry-count/size fields drive a large merged-list size, then observe SbpParseSignatureDatabase parse it via SbEFIGetVariable.
debugging_aids.expected_observation: Unpatched: the allocation size flows through lea/shl/add into SbeAlloc with no overflow check, then memmove copies the variable data. Patched: each multiply/add is validated; on overflow the helper returns STATUS_INTEGER_OVERFLOW (0xC0000095) and never allocates or copies.
debugging_aids.notes: RtlULongLongMult is absent from the entire unpatched binary and appears only in the patched build. Rated Low because a full 64-bit overflow of the allocation size is not demonstrable from these binaries (32-bit counts * 16 + 32-bit size, computed in 64-bit registers); the change is genuine CWE-190 integer-overflow hardening on an untrusted UEFI-variable parsing path.

summary: The primary security-relevant change tightens a System Integrity policy trust check in ci.dll (Windows Code Integrity). The unpatched SIPolicyIsPolicyPlatformSecured (sub_18006FC88) contains an early-return that marks a policy as 'platform secured' (return 1) when bit 2 of the policy-options flags at offset +0x710 is set and the function is called in non-strict mode (arg2=0), skipping the stricter check. The patched version removes this shortcut, so on the arg2=0 path it requires bit 0 set and a hardcoded policy-identity (0x4C0644C9A244370E / 0x7630566E01F651B5) at +0x6CC/+0x6D4 to match; the +0x710 offset is unchanged between builds and there is no page-hash table or binary search in this function. This is rated Medium (real trust gate, but attacker-reachability of the +0x710 bit-2 state is not demonstrable from the binaries). Separately, the patch removes one entry (0x4C3279F4784C4414 / 0xD1DA542FBF0A5A6 at a1[0]/a1[1]) from the SIPolicyIsPolicyMustBeWindowsSigned (sub_18006FB74) list of policy IDs that must be Windows-signed; the caller shows this RELAXES the requirement for that identity (it may now be accepted via customized signing), so it is informational, not a fix. It also adds a feature-gated flag check at +0x938 to the unrelated CiHvciIsVbsPolicyFailure (sub_18006C428) VBS/HVCI policy-failure path, and hardens the SHA-256 catalog hash lookup in I_FindFileOrHeaderHashInLoadedCatalogs (sub_1800D6FEC) by switching from bsearch to the bounds-checked bsearch_s. Separately, the patch hardens the EFI Secure Boot signature-database parser: unpatched SbpParseSignatureDatabase (0x18000BDDC) computed the merged-list allocation size with raw shl/add and no overflow check before SbeAlloc and a memmove of attacker-controlled UEFI-variable data (retrieved via SbEFIGetVariable, reached from SbParseKEK); the patch extracts this into the new helper SbpParseSignatureDatabaseFromBuffer (0x18001E1DC) and validates every multiplication with RtlULongLongMult and every addition with a carry check (returning STATUS_INTEGER_OVERFLOW) before allocating and copying. This is CWE-190 integer-overflow hardening guarding a CWE-787/CWE-122 heap overflow; rated Low because a demonstrable 64-bit overflow is not provable from these binaries (32-bit counts * 16 + 32-bit size, computed in 64-bit registers).
